evidence: Production code naming is uniformly camelCase throughout: handle*, build*, parse*, resolve* method prefixes applied consistently across all handler classes (SketchHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, ExplorationHandlers).

Package-private visibility applied consistently to all handler and internal helper classes (HttpApiUtils, VisualizationHandlers, BlackLabSnippetParser, CollocateQueryHelper, MultiSeedExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator), with public reserved for true API boundaries.
Utility classes (LogDiceUtils, MathUtils, CqlUtils, RelationPatternBuilder, RelationUtils, GrammarConfig, GrammarConfigLoader) all use `public final class` consistently.

Model package uses records for simple value objects (CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun, Edge, ExplorationOptions, FetchExamplesOptions, SingleSeedExplorationOptions, AdjectiveProfile) and classes for objects needing behaviour, with ExplorationResult carrying explicit Javadoc rationale for the exception.

RadialPlotTest.java and PatternSubstitutionTest.java both declare `package pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.api` but test classes from different packages: RadialPlot (pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.viz) and CqlUtils (pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.utils). Tests should mirror the package of the class under test.

HandlersTest.java is a catch-all mixing test cases for SketchHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, and ExplorationHandlers under the generic name 'Handlers'. Meanwhile the codebase has dedicated ExplorationHandlersTest, SketchHandlersTest, and CorpusQueryHandlersTest following the <ClassName>Test convention.

BCQLParserTest.java tests nl.inl.blacklab.queryParser.corpusql.CorpusQueryLanguageParser — a third-party class. The name BCQL uses an acronym not present in any production class name; the rest of the codebase uses BlackLab prefix (BlackLabQueryExecutor, BlackLabSnippetParser, BlackLabConllUIndexer).

ExploreResponseAssembler is declared `public final class` but is only referenced within the api package (ExplorationHandlers and api test classes). Sibling internal helper HttpApiUtils is correctly package-private.

HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling provides a single, centralized error boundary for all HTTP handlers: IAE→400, RequestEntityTooLargeException→413, IOException→500, catch-all Exception→500. Every endpoint in WordSketchApiServer.registerRoutes() is registered through this wrapper without exception.

IAE is used uniformly as the validation-failure signal throughout the stack: HttpApiUtils.requireParam, ExplorationHandlers (blank seed, bad relation, seeds < 2, nouns_per in multi), SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles (seeds < 2), CollocateProfileComparator.compareCollocateProfiles (null or < 2 seeds), GrammarConfigLoader (missing version, empty relations, duplicate id, bad positions), and BlackLabQueryExecutor.buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution (unrecognised pattern format).

BlackLabQueryExecutor constructor wraps BlackLab's ErrorOpeningIndex (RuntimeException) into a checked IOException with a descriptive message; executeCollocateSearch and executeCqlQuery both catch InvalidQuery and re-throw as IOException('BCQL parse error: ...'). CollocateQueryHelper.getTotalFrequency wraps any RuntimeException into IOException. This gives callers a predictable checked contract at the I/O boundary.

RequestEntityTooLargeException is a purpose-built exception type rather than overloading IAE for the 413 case — readBodyWithSizeLimit throws it on oversized bodies and wrapWithErrorHandling maps it to 413 separately from IAE, avoiding ambiguity about whether the client sent bad data or too much data.

BlackLabSnippetParser null-returning extraction methods (extractLemmaFromMatch, extractPosFromMatch, extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition, extractCollocateLemma) are all annotated @Nullable with documented semantics ('returns null if not found'). Callers consistently check for null or delegate to withFallback variants before using the results.

GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() uses a two-tier exception strategy: FileNotFoundException is wrapped in IllegalStateException with the config path in the message; generic IOException is wrapped separately. Both provide actionable messages for startup failures. The Javadoc explains the two-tier rationale explicitly.

CollocateQueryHelper declares its BlackLabIndex field as @Nullable with Objects.requireNonNull guards in getTotalFrequency and executeCollocateSearch — a test-seam pattern that embeds null-guard boilerplate into production methods. This adds noise without adding safety: production callers always pass a non-null index via BlackLabQueryExecutor, and the guard throws NPE rather than an informative exception.

SketchHandlers.executeRelationQueries catches IOException per relation and appends to a relationErrors list rather than propagating. The method comment describes this as intentional (don't fail the whole sketch for one bad relation), and the error strings are included in the response. However, the catch is broad — any IOException from executor calls is silently collected, which can obscure unexpected failures during development.

Handler methods use `handle*` prefix uniformly across all handler classes with precise domain suffixes: handleSemanticFieldExplore, handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti, handleSemanticFieldComparison, handleSemanticFieldExamples, handleSketchRequest, handleSurfaceRelations, handleDepRelations.

Recent renames (resolveRelationConfig, deriveDeprel, buildSketch, buildMultiSeedExplorationResult) have tightened name-to-behavior alignment — no vestige of generic predecessors remains in caller code.

SketchHandlers.java: `handleFullDependencySketch` (line 114) is a one-line passthrough to `handleDepSketchForType` (line 146). The `ForType` suffix on the inner method is inherited from its sibling `handleFullSketchForType` (which takes a `RelationType` parameter), but `handleDepSketchForType` takes no type parameter — DEP is hardcoded. The suffix suggests parameterizability that does not exist.

BlackLabQueryExecutor: `collectDependencyFrequencies` (private) not only collects frequencies but also calls `collocateQueryHelper.buildAndRankCollocates(...)` — it executes a full query-collect-rank pipeline. The name covers only the first stage.

ExploreResponseAssembler: `buildEdges` is overloaded for `ExplorationResult` (produces SEED_ADJ + DISCOVERED_ADJ edges) and `ComparisonResult` (produces MODIFIER edges). The two overloads construct structurally different graph outputs; the shared name does not hint at the difference.
handleFullDependencySketch in SketchHandlers is a single-line pass-through: `handleDepSketchForType(exchange, lemma)` with no added logic; its Javadoc restates what the delegate does

CollocateProfileComparator.compareCollocateProfiles logs both input parameters at entry: `logger.debug("Nouns: {}", seedNouns)` and `logger.debug("Min logDice: {}", minLogDice)` — input-echo logging at entry

CollocateProfileComparator.buildRawAdjectiveProfiles emits three debug lines per noun inside the loop: entry log ("\nProfiling: {}"), count log ("Found {} adjectives"), and top-5 dump (`logger.debug("  Top 5: {}", adjectives.subList(...))`)
GrammarConfigLoader.missingConfigException(Path p) is a single-line private factory used exactly once in readConfigFile — over-extracted for a trivial message

WordSketchApiServer registers `/api/semantic-field` as a legacy alias for `/api/semantic-field/compare` with a `/* @deprecated */` comment; the endpoint is live and functional with no server-side warning log when it is hit
ConcordanceHandlers.handleConcordanceExamples Javadoc records `formerly word1` and `formerly word2` in parameter documentation — historical traces of a completed rename that persist in comments
No TODO/FIXME comments referencing 'migrate', 'legacy', 'old api', or 'remove after' found in the codebase
All class-move migrations described in context notes are complete: QueryResults in model/, GrammarConfigSerializer in api/, options records in model/
incomplete_migration.issues_preventing_higher_score: The legacy /api/semantic-field endpoint lacks a deprecation warning log on invocation, making client usage invisible and complicating safe removal planning. The `formerly word1`/`formerly word2` documentation traces are trivial but leave stale historical context in the Javadoc.

evidence: ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExplore and handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti both call `relationConfig.relationType().get().name()` with a comment `// Safe: resolveRelationConfig guarantees relationType is present`; using Optional.get() with a safety comment rather than .orElseThrow() buries the contract in a comment instead of code

CorpusQueryHandlers.parseBcqlRequest: `Optional.ofNullable(obj.get("top")).map(v -> obj.getIntValue("top")).filter(v -> v > 0).orElse(10)` — the lambda `v -> obj.getIntValue("top")` ignores its argument `v` and calls getIntValue("top") by key again; the presence-check (via ofNullable) and the value-retrieval (via getIntValue) are needlessly decoupled, making the intent unclear

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern (package-private overload): `if (seed.isEmpty())` throws IAE with message 'seed must not be blank' but there is no prior null guard; passing null throws NullPointerException with a misleading stack trace

CollocateProfileComparator.compareCollocateProfiles: null check `if (seedNouns == null || seedNouns.size() < 2)` is redundant — the single production caller (SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles) validates size >= 2 before delegating and never passes null

ExploreResponseAssembler.populateComparisonResponse takes (Map, ComparisonResult, Set<String>, int, double) — 5 parameters; sibling populateExploreResponse takes (Map, ExplorationResult) — 2 parameters; the extra three (seeds, topCollocates, minLogDice) are echoed into the response's 'parameters' sub-map, duplicating caller context

ConcordanceHandlers: HTTP params 'seed' and 'collocate' are silently renamed to 'noun' and 'adjective' in the ConcordanceExamplesRequest record; the Javadoc explains the rename but the surface-to-domain mismatch requires callers to reconcile two naming schemes

CollocateQueryHelper.executeCollocateSearch(String, String, boolean withStoredHits): the boolean parameter toggles between groupWithStoredHits and group execution paths — boolean toggle behaviour is opaque at call sites

QueryExecutor.executeCqlQuery names its pattern parameter `cqlPattern` while executeSurfacePattern names its `bcqlPattern`; the distinction is meaningful (different parsers) but the asymmetric naming is easily confused
252 tests passing; 5 @Disabled (live BlackLab index tests in BlackLabQueryExecutorTest, BlackLabBcqlConcordanceTest, etc.) — the full index→HTTP path is never exercised in CI

MockExchange inner class is copy-pasted separately into HandlersTest, SketchHandlersTest, ExplorationHandlersTest, CorpusQueryHandlersTest, VisualizationHandlersTest — 5+ independent implementations of the same 25-line boilerplate; any JDK HttpExchange API change requires updating all copies

HandlersTest.java (24 KB, 25+ tests) is an omnibus test class covering ConcordanceHandlers, SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, VisualizationHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers AND edge-weight comparison logic; it substantially overlaps with SketchHandlersTest, ExplorationHandlersTest, CorpusQueryHandlersTest, VisualizationHandlersTest which were added later as focused companions
GrammarConfigSerializer has no test: toJson(GrammarConfig) and toJson(RelationConfig) convert the core config model to JSON but are exercised only implicitly through handler round-trips
QueryExecutor interface is used correctly as a testability seam — CollocateQueryHelperTest, CollocateProfileComparatorTest, MultiSeedExplorerTest all use clean stubs without touching the index
Handler tests assert response shape (relations map, collocations nested under relation ID, seeds array, adjectives_count, seed_count) — not just HTTP status codes
WordSketchApiServerTest starts a real HTTP server on port 18080 and fires real HTTP requests — partial integration coverage at the routing layer
CollocateQueryHelper is well-tested with a subclass stub pattern for the frequency-lookup seam, covering XML extraction, logDice ranking, and per-position extraction
Dependency direction is clean: model → utils → config/query → exploration → api; no reverse dependencies detected
QueryExecutor is in query package but is imported by api, exploration, and config — it functions as a shared port/seam rather than a cycle

WordSketchApiServer has a convenience constructor 'public WordSketchApiServer(QueryExecutor executor, int port, GrammarConfig grammarConfig)' that constructs SemanticFieldExplorer internally, embedding exploration-layer wiring responsibility inside the api package

api package contains HttpApiUtils, RequestEntityTooLargeException, all handler classes, WordSketchApiServer, ExploreResponseAssembler, and GrammarConfigSerializer — HTTP infrastructure concerns and presentation-assembly concerns coexist without a sub-package boundary

SemanticFieldExplorer (exploration) is a facade that owns single-seed exploration directly and delegates to MultiSeedExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator — clean internal decomposition with package-private collaborators

RelationUtils (config) is called from CollocateProfileComparator (exploration) via RelationUtils.findBestCollocatePattern — the exploration→config direction is expected but RelationUtils has generic utility methods that would fit equally in utils
No import cycles detected across the six main packages: api, config, exploration, indexer, model, query, utils
scan_evidence coupling boundary_violations is empty — no mechanical boundary violations flagged

WordSketchApiServer Javadoc explicitly documents the security model: 'the server binds exclusively to the loopback address (127.0.0.1) so it is never exposed on any external network interface. All endpoints are unauthenticated — access control is provided by the host firewall and the loopback-only bind.'

CORS headers are applied via HttpApiUtils.setCorsHeader() which is called by every response-sending method (sendJsonResponse, sendError, sendOptionsResponse, sendBinaryResponse) — no endpoint can bypass the CORS header
POST endpoints consistently enforce a 65 KB body size limit via HttpApiUtils.readBodyWithSizeLimit: CorpusQueryHandlers passes maxBytes=65_536, VisualizationHandlers passes maxBytes=65_536
GET endpoints accept arbitrary-length query parameter values (seed, seeds, lemma, adjective, noun) with no length constraint; a very long seed string is passed to BlackLab without truncation

All routes are wrapped with HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling, ensuring that IllegalArgumentException (user input errors) maps to 400 and runtime exceptions to 500 consistently — no endpoint can leak an unhandled exception

CorpusQueryHandlers validates CQL pattern complexity (bracket depth > 20 → 400) in addition to JSON validity and field presence — additional trust-boundary hardening beyond the standard requireParam check
No admin vs. regular endpoint distinction exists — there are no privileged operations or bypass paths that would require asymmetric trust enforcement
The /health endpoint is a simple unauthenticated ping with no access to corpus data — correctly excluded from any validation concern
authorization_consistency.issues_preventing_higher_score: Score is 82 — below 85 — so this field is not required.

strengths: Production package naming and class visibility are uniformly applied: package-private for internals, public only at true API boundaries, no drift across packages.
Method naming prefixes (handle*, build*, parse*, resolve*) are applied consistently across all four handler classes.
Utility classes uniformly follow the `public final class` + private constructor pattern, with no outliers.
Mixed record/class strategy in the model package is documented where it deviates from the record-first default (ExplorationResult carries explicit rationale Javadoc).
convention_outlier.issue_character: Conventions are solid in production code but the test tree has three localised outliers: two test files placed in the wrong package, one catch-all handler test that mixes concerns against the prevailing per-class test convention, and one test named after a non-existent production class.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: The production codebase scores highly on convention consistency — naming, visibility, and file organisation are uniform across all packages. The penalty comes from the test layer, where RadialPlotTest and PatternSubstitutionTest are misrouted to the api test package rather than viz and utils respectively, HandlersTest is a residual catch-all that coexists with dedicated per-class test files (a style island in the test tree), and BCQLParserTest uses an acronym convention not present elsewhere. These are genuine outliers against the prevailing convention but are limited to the test layer and do not affect runtime behaviour, placing this in the 70–75 range on a codebase that is otherwise well-organised.

strengths: wrapWithErrorHandling is a single, centralized API error boundary used by every registered endpoint — no handler bypasses it, so all exceptions are mapped to HTTP status codes in one place with consistent log levels (warn for 400/413, error for 500).

IllegalArgumentException is used as the consistent signal for client/validation errors at every layer — parameter parsing, relation resolution, seed cardinality checks, config validation — and the mapping from IAE to HTTP 400 is documented explicitly in method Javadocs.

RuntimeException-to-IOException wrapping at the BlackLab boundary is uniform: ErrorOpeningIndex in the constructor, InvalidQuery in two query methods, and RuntimeException in getTotalFrequency all produce IOException with context-preserving cause chains and descriptive messages.
RequestEntityTooLargeException cleanly separates 'too much data' (413) from 'bad data' (400/IAE), avoiding the ambiguity of overloading IllegalArgumentException for size violations.

@Nullable annotations and 'returns null if not found' contracts in BlackLabSnippetParser are consistently applied and respected by callers — this is appropriate for XML-parsing extraction helpers where absence is normal, not exceptional.
error_consistency.issue_character: Issues are localized rather than systemic: the design intent is clearly uniform (throw IAE for validation, IOException for I/O, one centralized boundary), but two points deviate — a test-seam pattern that introduces nullable-field boilerplate into production null-guard code, and an intentional-but-broad IOException swallowing loop in the sketch relation aggregator.
error_consistency.score_rationale: The codebase's error handling architecture is clearly designed and consistently applied at all the boundary points that matter most: the HTTP layer has a single deterministic mapping, the validation layer speaks one exception language (IAE), and the query layer wraps unpredictable third-party exceptions into a predictable contract. This pushes the score well above 60. The deductions come from two real but limited deviations: the nullable-index test seam in CollocateQueryHelper adds null-guard noise in production methods and makes the field contract weaker than necessary; and executeRelationQueries broadly catches IOException for resilience in a way that can obscure unexpected query failures during development. These are module-scoped concerns that do not undermine the architecture, keeping the score below the 80 'solid but uneven' threshold.

strengths: `handle*` prefix is applied uniformly across all four handler classes with specific domain suffixes, letting a developer locate the right handler without reading bodies.
Factory methods consistently use `build*`, parse methods use `parse*`, and alias-resolution methods use `resolve*` — vocabulary is stable and domain-aligned throughout the codebase.
Domain terms (collocate, deprel, logDice, seed, minShared) are used consistently and match the linguistics domain, with no invented synonyms or conflated concepts.
Recent renames (resolveRelationConfig, deriveDeprel, buildSketch, buildMultiSeedExplorationResult) demonstrate active improvement of name precision — the direction of travel is good.
Records are well-named: ExplorationParams, ExecutedSketch, SeedCollocateData all communicate their held structure at a glance.
naming_quality.issue_character: The issues are isolated to private implementation details — one vestigial suffix, one underselling private method name, one overloaded name masking distinct graph semantics — rather than a systemic naming convention failure.
naming_quality.score_rationale: A developer reading this codebase can correctly predict behaviour from names the large majority of the time; the public API surface and handler layer are especially clean. The few friction points (handleDepSketchForType's misleading suffix, collectDependencyFrequencies underselling its ranking step, buildEdges overloaded for structurally different outputs) are confined to private or package-private methods and require only single-edit fixes. This sits comfortably above the 80 anchor ('solid but uneven') because the issues are isolated rather than recurring, but below 90 because each identified friction point represents a real moment where a developer reading the code would be briefly misled.

strengths: Domain vocabulary is precise throughout: `exploreByPattern`, `buildCollocateToNounsMap`, `filterByMinShared`, `identifyCoreCollocates` — no handleData/processItem/doOperation generics
Comments explain WHY rather than restating code: constructor I/O note in WordSketchApiServer, rotation strategy in ConlluConverter, thread-interrupt handling in Main all add genuine context
`wrapWithErrorHandling` genuinely adds error-mapping logic (RequestEntityTooLargeException → 413, IAE → 400, IOException → 500) — not a pass-through
Debug logging is guarded appropriately: `logTopProfiles` checks `logger.isDebugEnabled()` before expensive operations
Wrapper methods that bind a constant argument (handleSurfaceRelations → handleRelationsForType(exchange, RelationType.SURFACE)) serve as named route-registration points, not dead indirection
ai_generated_debt.issue_character: Issues are isolated and mild — one clear pass-through private method and per-iteration input-echo debug logging in the profiler loop, not a systemic pattern across the codebase.
ai_generated_debt.score_rationale: The codebase avoids the worst AI-debt markers: domain vocabulary is precise, comments add insight, and wrappers generally add logic. The per-noun debug loop in CollocateProfileComparator (three logger.debug calls per iteration) and the one-line pass-through `handleFullDependencySketch` are genuine AI-hallmark patterns but limited in scope. This places the code in the solid-but-uneven range (75–82), closer to the upper end because the issues are few and localized rather than systemic.

strengths: All class-move migrations mentioned in context are complete: no residual references to old package locations or class names found
The legacy `/api/semantic-field` endpoint is clearly documented with a deprecation comment and canonical-path pointer (`Use /api/semantic-field/compare instead`)
The `word1`/`word2` → `seed`/`collocate` HTTP parameter rename was completed without leaving any backward-compat code for the old names
No stale migration TODO/FIXME markers present in any seed file
incomplete_migration.issue_character: The codebase has one intentionally retained legacy endpoint and two trivial documentation traces; there are no cases of old-and-new API patterns genuinely coexisting with active callers.
incomplete_migration.score_rationale: The codebase is in excellent shape for migration debt: all class-move migrations are complete, there are no mixed old/new patterns, and the single retained legacy endpoint is documented. The score falls just below exemplary because the legacy endpoint has no runtime deprecation signal (warning log on invocation), which makes it impossible to know from server logs alone whether any client still relies on it — a small but real gap in migration hygiene.

strengths: Guard clauses used consistently across all HTTP handlers: invalid input throws IAE immediately rather than propagating partial state
SemanticFieldExplorer divides the single-seed algorithm into four clearly labelled phases (// ========= EXPLORATION PHASE HELPERS =========), each in a well-named private method
ConlluConverter documents its key invariant explicitly: `// Invariant: inSentence implies writer != null` in the processLines Javadoc — and the code upholds it
Branching in handleSketchRequest cleanly separates the dep/surface/full/single-relation paths with early return, no nested if-else
logic_clarity.issue_character: Issues cluster around two patterns: Optional.get() called with a comment-based safety assertion (rather than code-enforced contract via orElseThrow), and one over-engineered Optional chain whose lambda ignores its parameter — both in the API layer rather than the algorithmic core.
logic_clarity.score_rationale: The algorithmic core (SemanticFieldExplorer phases, ConlluConverter, CollocateProfileComparator scoring) has clear, correct control flow. The API layer introduces two logic-clarity defects: Optional.get() appearing twice with safety-comment workarounds, and a redundant Optional chain whose lambda silently ignores its argument. These are not subtle bugs but they require a reader to hold extra context to verify correctness. The redundant null check and missing null guard in exploreByPattern are additional minor issues. Together these pull the score below 80 but the codebase remains above the significant-drag threshold (60), landing in the 70–75 range.

strengths: QueryExecutor interface has consistent parameter ordering across all execute* methods: (lemma, pattern/deprel, minLogDice, maxResults) — callers can orient immediately

Options objects (ExplorationOptions, SingleSeedExplorationOptions, FetchExamplesOptions) used uniformly for the exploration API, making the SemanticFieldExplorer public surface extensible without parameter-count growth
Handler classes are cleanly decomposed into focused concerns (SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, VisualizationHandlers) with no cross-concern method calls
QueryExecutor Javadoc documents error contracts per method: IOException for index failures, IAE for pattern format errors
api_surface_coherence.issue_character: Issues concentrate at the assembler/handler boundary rather than in the core domain API: the response-assembly layer has asymmetric method signatures and an HTTP-to-domain naming gap, while the core query and exploration APIs are coherent.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: The core APIs (QueryExecutor, SemanticFieldExplorer, options records) are well-shaped and internally consistent. The coherence deficit lives in the response assembly and HTTP-parsing layer: populateComparisonResponse takes 5 raw parameters where its sibling takes 2, and ConcordanceHandlers silently renames HTTP params to domain terms. The boolean-toggle in CollocateQueryHelper.executeCollocateSearch further obscures call-site intent. These are real friction points for API consumers but are limited to specific files. The codebase falls in the 70–78 range — solid core but uneven presentation layer.

strengths: QueryExecutor interface used consistently as a stub seam — exploration, handler, and query tests all isolate from the live index cleanly
Handler tests assert response structure (key presence, value types, nested shapes) rather than only HTTP status codes
Zero snapshot tests — all assertions are behavioural and won't become stale on unrelated refactors
WordSketchApiServerTest provides real HTTP round-trip coverage at the routing/lifecycle layer without requiring a corpus index
CollocateQueryHelperTest uses a protected-override stub pattern to verify logDice ranking and XML extraction in isolation from BlackLab
test_strategy.issue_character: The test suite shows visible evolutionary layering: an omnibus HandlersTest coexists with four newer focused files covering overlapping ground, and the MockExchange boilerplate has been copy-pasted into every test file rather than extracted — indicating growth without refactoring rather than a systemic design flaw.
test_strategy.score_rationale: The breadth of coverage is genuine: 252 tests span handlers, config loading, exploration algorithms, CQL utilities, and index helpers. The QueryExecutor seam and stub patterns are correctly applied throughout. However, the suite carries structural drag: MockExchange is duplicated 5+ times, HandlersTest overlaps substantially with its four focused siblings, GrammarConfigSerializer has no test, and all live-index tests are permanently @Disabled leaving the full BlackLab production path unverified in CI. This places the codebase above 'significant drag' (60) — coverage is real — but below 'solid with minor gaps' (75) because the fragility from MockExchange duplication and the @Disabled critical-path tests are genuine risks that would hurt a developer maintaining this suite.

strengths: Clean single-direction dependency flow: model and utils are foundations, config and query build on them, exploration composes config+query, api orchestrates all — no upward imports
QueryExecutor interface provides a proper inversion point between the api/exploration layers and the BlackLab infrastructure, enabling stub-based testing without architectural gymnastics

exploration package decomposes its algorithms correctly: SemanticFieldExplorer is a public facade, MultiSeedExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator are package-private collaborators — callers see one stable surface
model package is a pure DTO layer with zero outgoing imports to other project packages — it cannot be a cycle source
cross_module_architecture.issue_character: The issues are containment gaps rather than boundary violations: the api package has grown to include both HTTP infrastructure and presentation-assembly responsibilities without a sub-boundary, and the convenience constructor in WordSketchApiServer pulls domain-object construction into the API layer — both symptoms of incremental growth in a single-package hub.
cross_module_architecture.score_rationale: The codebase has no import cycles, a well-directed dependency graph, and good use of package-private visibility to hide internal collaborators. This is solidly above 'significant drag' (60). What prevents a higher score is the api package acting as a broad hub that mixes HTTP mechanics (HttpApiUtils, handler classes, WordSketchApiServer) with response assembly (ExploreResponseAssembler) and config serialization (GrammarConfigSerializer) without any sub-package structure, and the convenience constructor embedding SemanticFieldExplorer construction inside WordSketchApiServer. Neither is a cycle, but both widen the blast radius of api-layer changes. A score of 72 — 'solid layering with visible boundary gaps that do not yet cause coupling problems' — is appropriate.

strengths: Security model is explicitly documented in WordSketchApiServer Javadoc: loopback-only bind, no auth by design, with a clear note that container deployment would require an additional auth layer
CORS header is applied through a single shared method (setCorsHeader) called inside every response path — impossible to accidentally omit it on a new endpoint
POST endpoints uniformly enforce 65 KB body size limits, JSON validity, required-field presence, and business-logic guards (CQL bracket depth) — no sibling POST endpoint skips these checks
wrapWithErrorHandling wraps every route handler, providing consistent exception→HTTP status mapping across all endpoints without per-handler error handling code
authorization_consistency.issue_character: The sole notable inconsistency is that POST endpoints apply an explicit size gate (65 KB limit) and structured validation while GET endpoints accept unbounded query parameter values — a natural HTTP idiom difference, but one that creates an asymmetric trust boundary between the two groups of endpoints.
authorization_consistency.score_rationale: This is a local research tool explicitly designed as unauthenticated, and the security model is consistently applied: all endpoints are equally unauthenticated, CORS is uniformly set, and error handling is uniformly wrapped. The dimension's rubric concerns (some routes with auth, others without; permission magic strings; asymmetric input validation) are largely non-applicable or cleanly handled. The score sits at 82 rather than 90+ because GET endpoints accept unbounded parameter inputs while POST endpoints have explicit size limits — a minor but real asymmetry in input trust boundaries that a developer adding a new GET endpoint would have no guidance to address.

issues.summary: RadialPlotTest and PatternSubstitutionTest declared in api test package but test classes from viz and utils packages
issues.suggestion: Move RadialPlotTest to src/test/java/pl/marcinmilkowski/word_sketch/viz/ and PatternSubstitutionTest to src/test/java/pl/marcinmilkowski/word_sketch/utils/ (renaming it CqlUtilsTest or PatternSubstitutionTest under utils to match the tested class). Create the target directories if they don't exist.
evidence: RadialPlotTest.java line 1: `package pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.api;` — imports and tests `pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.viz.RadialPlot`.
PatternSubstitutionTest.java line 1: `package pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.api;` — imports and tests `pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.utils.CqlUtils`.
The prevailing convention places tests in the package matching their subject: BlackLabSnippetParserTest is in query/, LogDiceUtilsTest is in utils/, etc.

issues.summary: HandlersTest is a catch-all mixing tests for three distinct handler classes, coexisting with dedicated per-class test files
issues.suggestion: Distribute HandlersTest cases into the corresponding dedicated test files (SketchHandlersTest, ExplorationHandlersTest, ConcordanceHandlersTest) and delete HandlersTest. This eliminates the style island and consolidates coverage in predictable locations.
evidence: HandlersTest.java contains sections marked `// --- SketchHandlers tests ---`, `// --- ExplorationHandlers tests ---`, and tests ConcordanceHandlers — covering three distinct classes in one file.
The prevailing convention is one test class per production class: ExplorationHandlersTest, SketchHandlersTest, CorpusQueryHandlersTest all exist as dedicated files.
No production class named 'Handlers' exists; the test file name does not follow the <ClassName>Test convention.

issues.summary: BCQLParserTest uses an acronym convention absent from production code and names a test after a non-existent production class
issues.suggestion: Rename to BlackLabCqlParserTest (or CorpusQueryLanguageParserTest) to align with the BlackLab-prefix convention used across the query package. Update the class declaration and file name accordingly.
evidence: BCQLParserTest.java: `public class BCQLParserTest` — no production class named BCQLParser exists in src/main.
The test exercises `nl.inl.blacklab.queryParser.corpusql.CorpusQueryLanguageParser` (a third-party BlackLab class).
Sibling production classes all use the BlackLab prefix: BlackLabQueryExecutor, BlackLabSnippetParser, BlackLabConllUIndexer — not the BCQL acronym.

issues.summary: ExploreResponseAssembler declared public but is only consumed within the api package
issues.suggestion: Change `public final class ExploreResponseAssembler` to `final class ExploreResponseAssembler` (package-private) to match the visibility convention of other internal api helpers. Verify that no test outside the api package references it before making the change.
evidence: ExploreResponseAssembler.java line 27: `public final class ExploreResponseAssembler`.
All references to ExploreResponseAssembler are within the api package (ExplorationHandlers.java and api test classes).

Sibling internal helper HttpApiUtils is correctly declared package-private: `class HttpApiUtils` (line 32). Handler classes (SketchHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, VisualizationHandlers) are also package-private.
GrammarConfigSerializer is legitimately public because GrammarConfigLoaderTest in the config test package imports it.

issues.summary: CollocateQueryHelper's @Nullable BlackLabIndex field embeds null-guard boilerplate into production methods as a test-seam pattern
issues.suggestion: Remove the @Nullable annotation and the Objects.requireNonNull guards from the production field and methods. Instead, introduce a package-private constructor or factory method (or a test-only subclass) that accepts a nullable/mock executor, keeping the null-contract concern in test infrastructure rather than in production code.
evidence: The constructor Javadoc states: '@Nullable BlackLabIndex index accepted only in test subclasses that override getTotalFrequency and executeCollocateSearch — all production callers must supply a non-null index.'

Objects.requireNonNull(index, "BlackLabIndex must not be null for getTotalFrequency") and Objects.requireNonNull(index, "BlackLabIndex must not be null for executeCollocateSearch") appear in production methods solely to defend against the test-seam null.
Production path (BlackLabQueryExecutor constructor) always supplies a non-null index, making the null branch unreachable in production — the guard is only meaningful for test subclasses.

issues.summary: executeRelationQueries broadly catches IOException per relation into an error list, masking unexpected query failures behind resilience intent
issues.suggestion: Consider narrowing the caught exception to a more specific type, or add a separate counter/severity field to distinguish expected empty-results from actual I/O failures in the response. At minimum, document the intended boundary in the method comment so maintainers don't inadvertently widen the catch further.
evidence: In executeRelationQueries, the catch block is: catch (IOException e) { logger.warn(...); relationErrors.add(rel.id() + ": " + e.getMessage()); } — the original exception is not re-thrown or wrapped.

The catch covers any IOException from executor.executeSurfacePattern or executor.executeDependencyPatternWithPos, including transient failures, index corruption, and protocol errors — not just expected 'no results' scenarios.

The warn-level log and error-list-in-response approach means a serious but transient failure (e.g. index corruption on one relation) returns a 200 with partial results and an 'errors' array, indistinguishable from a normal 'no collocates found' response unless the caller inspects the errors field.

issues.summary: handleDepSketchForType has a vestigial `ForType` suffix — it takes no RelationType parameter, unlike its sibling
issues.suggestion: Rename handleDepSketchForType to handleFullDepSketch (matching the domain term already used in the Javadoc comment at line 148), then inline or remove the handleFullDependencySketch passthrough so the call from handleSketchRequest calls handleFullDepSketch directly.
evidence: handleFullSketchForType (line 118) takes a RelationType parameter — the `ForType` suffix is meaningful there.
handleDepSketchForType (line 146) takes no type parameter; DEP is hardcoded inside. The suffix implies parameterizability that does not exist.
handleFullDependencySketch (line 114) is a one-line passthrough to handleDepSketchForType — both are private, the delegation adds no value and doubles the naming surface.

issues.summary: collectDependencyFrequencies (BlackLabQueryExecutor) names only frequency collection but also performs ranking via buildAndRankCollocates
issues.suggestion: Rename to executeDepCollocateQuery or queryAndRankDepCollocates to convey the full execute-collect-rank pipeline rather than just the intermediate collection step.
evidence: Method name suggests a collection step, but the final statement calls collocateQueryHelper.buildAndRankCollocates(freqMap, headwordFreq, minLogDice, maxResults, lemmaPosMap) — a full ranking pass with minLogDice filtering.
The counterpart collectFrequenciesFromGroups (also in the same class) does only collect and does not rank, making the naming asymmetry visible.

issues.summary: handleFullDependencySketch is a pure single-line delegation with a restating docstring
issues.suggestion: Inline the call into handleSketchRequest's dispatch block where it is the only caller, or collapse handleDepSketchForType into this method if the naming distinction adds no value
evidence: Method body: `handleDepSketchForType(exchange, lemma);` — no added logic

Javadoc says 'Handle full dependency sketch request. Returns all dependency relations for the given lemma. DEP relations use surface patterns with [deprel=...] constraints.' — this restates what handleDepSketchForType does

issues.summary: CollocateProfileComparator.buildRawAdjectiveProfiles emits three debug lines per noun in a hot loop
issues.suggestion: Collect a per-noun summary in a local list and emit a single `logger.debug("Profiled {} nouns: {}", count, summary)` after the loop, matching the style of SemanticFieldExplorer which logs entry and completion summaries rather than per-item entries
evidence: `logger.debug("\nProfiling: {}", noun)` on every noun
`logger.debug("  Found {} adjectives", adjectives.size())` on every noun
`logger.debug("  Top 5: {}", adjectives.subList(0, Math.min(5, adjectives.size())))` on every noun — dumps a list object per iteration

issues.summary: Legacy /api/semantic-field endpoint has no warning log on invocation, making active client usage invisible
issues.suggestion: Wrap the legacy handler with a one-line adapter that logs `logger.warn("Deprecated endpoint /api/semantic-field called — use /api/semantic-field/compare instead")` before delegating, or add a call count metric; this makes deprecation progress observable without changing client behaviour
evidence: Route registered as `/* @deprecated legacy alias for /api/semantic-field/compare; kept for backward compatibility. */` but the handler `explorationHandlers::handleSemanticFieldComparison` is unwrapped — no log statement fires when the legacy path is called
Without a warning log, operators cannot determine from server logs whether any client still uses the deprecated path and therefore cannot schedule its removal safely

issues.summary: Optional.get() called with a safety comment rather than .orElseThrow() in two handler methods
issues.suggestion: Replace both occurrences with `.orElseThrow(() -> new IllegalStateException("relationType unexpectedly absent after resolveRelationConfig — this is a bug"))`. This makes the invariant code-visible and produces an actionable message if it is ever violated.
evidence: handleSemanticFieldExplore: `// Safe: resolveRelationConfig guarantees relationType is present` followed by `String relationType = relationConfig.relationType().get().name();`
handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti: same pattern — `// Safe: ...` then `.get().name()`
The contract lives in the comment, not in code; a future refactor of resolveRelationConfig that returns an empty Optional would produce NoSuchElementException with no informative message

issues.summary: Optional chain in parseBcqlRequest has a lambda that ignores its argument, obscuring intent
issues.suggestion: Simplify to: `int rawTop = obj.getIntValue("top", 0); int resolvedTop = rawTop > 0 ? rawTop : 10;` — this is two lines, immediately readable, and uses the Fastjson2 default-value overload that already handles absent keys.
evidence: `Optional.ofNullable(obj.get("top")).map(v -> obj.getIntValue("top")).filter(v -> v > 0).orElse(10)`

The lambda `v -> obj.getIntValue("top")` receives the unwrapped JSON value `v` but discards it and calls `obj.getIntValue("top")` again by key — the presence-check (ofNullable) and the value-read (getIntValue) are decoupled with no clarity benefit

issues.summary: exploreByPattern (package-private) calls seed.isEmpty() without a prior null check
issues.suggestion: Add `Objects.requireNonNull(seed, "seed must not be null");` before the isEmpty() check, or replace with `if (seed == null || seed.isEmpty())` and a single IAE — consistent with how other validators in this codebase handle null+blank together.
evidence: Method signature has no @NonNull annotation on `seed`
First statement: `if (seed.isEmpty()) { throw new IllegalArgumentException("seed must not be blank"); }` — null input throws NullPointerException, not the promised IllegalArgumentException
The misleading NPE stack trace would point into isEmpty() with message 'seed must not be blank' in context

issues.summary: populateComparisonResponse takes 5 parameters while populateExploreResponse takes 2, creating an asymmetric assembler API
issues.suggestion: Pass an ExplorationOptions (which already holds topCollocates and minLogDice) as the third parameter and derive seeds from ComparisonResult.nouns(), or add a seeds field to ComparisonResult. This aligns the signature with populateExploreResponse and removes the raw-primitive parameters.
evidence: `populateExploreResponse(Map<String,Object> response, ExplorationResult result)` — 2 params
`populateComparisonResponse(Map<String,Object> response, ComparisonResult result, Set<String> seeds, int topCollocates, double minLogDice)` — 5 params
The extra three params (seeds, topCollocates, minLogDice) are inserted into `response.put("parameters", paramsUsed)` — they echo the caller's already-available ExplorationOptions context

issues.summary: ConcordanceHandlers silently renames HTTP params 'seed'/'collocate' to domain terms 'noun'/'adjective' with no API surface indicator
issues.suggestion: Either: (a) align the record field names with the HTTP parameter names (noun → seed, adjective → collocate) since the HTTP contract is the API surface, and any semantic narrowing (these are specifically a noun and an adjective) can live in a comment; or (b) accept domain names but add them to the endpoint Javadoc as explicit field mapping so the rename is visible in one place
evidence: HTTP parameter: `seed` → ConcordanceExamplesRequest field: `noun`
HTTP parameter: `collocate` → ConcordanceExamplesRequest field: `adjective`

The Javadoc comment explains the renaming, but the rename means endpoint documentation (seed/collocate) and the handler code (noun/adjective) speak different languages — a reader tracing from the API call to domain logic must mentally translate

issues.summary: CollocateQueryHelper.executeCollocateSearch uses a boolean to toggle between two execution paths, making call sites opaque
issues.suggestion: Replace the boolean with two named methods: `executeCollocateSearchWithStoredHits(String, String)` and `executeCollocateSearch(String, String)`, or introduce a `StoredHitsMode` enum (STORED, LIGHTWEIGHT). Either approach makes the intent legible at each call site.
evidence: Signature: `CollocateSearch executeCollocateSearch(String lemma, String bcqlPattern, boolean withStoredHits)`

Call sites: `collocateQueryHelper.executeCollocateSearch(lemma, bcql, true)` (in executeCollocations, collectDependencyFrequencies) and `collocateQueryHelper.executeCollocateSearch(lemma, bcqlPattern, false)` (in executeSurfacePattern)
A reader seeing `executeCollocateSearch(lemma, bcql, true)` cannot determine what `true` means without inspecting the method signature or documentation

issues.summary: MockExchange inner class copy-pasted into 5+ test files instead of being shared
issues.suggestion: Extract a shared MockHttpExchange (and PostMockHttpExchange) into a test-scoped utility class in the api test package, e.g. src/test/java/pl/marcinmilkowski/word_sketch/api/MockHttpExchange.java, and have all handler test classes reference it.
evidence: Each file contains a nearly-identical 25-line MockExchange class implementing all abstract HttpExchange methods — getRequestURI, getRequestHeaders, getResponseHeaders, getRequestMethod, getRequestBody, getResponseBody, sendResponseHeaders, close, etc.
CorpusQueryHandlersTest and VisualizationHandlersTest also define a separate PostMockExchange subclass for POST bodies — both are also duplicated independently
Any new abstract method added to com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpExchange (or a JDK upgrade changing the signature) would require updating all 5+ copies

issues.summary: HandlersTest omnibus class substantially overlaps with four newer focused test files
issues.suggestion: Decide whether HandlersTest should be the canonical home for cross-handler integration tests (e.g. wrapWithErrorHandling interactions) while the focused *Test files own happy-path and edge cases — then deduplicate by removing redundant assertions from one location. Alternatively, migrate everything to focused files and retire HandlersTest.
evidence: HandlersTest.java (24 KB, 25+ @Test methods) covers handleConcordanceExamples (3 tests), handleSemanticFieldExamples (3 tests), handleSketchRequest (3 tests), handleSemanticFieldComparison (3 tests), handleVisualRadial (3 tests), handleCorpusQuery (4 tests)
CorpusQueryHandlersTest has 5 tests for handleCorpusQuery — 3 of which (invalidJson, missingQueryField, blankQuery) duplicate tests already in HandlersTest
VisualizationHandlersTest has 4 tests for handleVisualRadial — bodyTooLarge and invalidJson overlap with HandlersTest
Duplication means a logic change in one handler may require updating test assertions in two test files

issues.summary: GrammarConfigSerializer.toJson has no direct test coverage
issues.suggestion: Add a GrammarConfigSerializerTest that loads the test grammar config via GrammarConfigHelper.requireTestConfig(), calls toJson(config), and asserts key fields (version, relations array, id, pattern, relation_type) are correctly serialized; also test a minimal RelationConfig with null optional fields to verify they are omitted.
evidence: GrammarConfigSerializer.toJson(GrammarConfig) and toJson(RelationConfig) are the sole callers that convert the config model to JSON for API responses
No test class for GrammarConfigSerializer exists; it is only exercised indirectly through live server integration tests
The method has branching logic (optional fields: name, description, pattern, relationType) that could silently omit fields on null inputs

issues.summary: All live-index integration tests are permanently @Disabled, leaving the full BlackLab pipeline unverified in CI
issues.suggestion: Create a small synthetic CoNLL-U test corpus (20–50 sentences) as a Maven test resource and build a minimal BlackLab index from it in a @BeforeAll fixture; this would unblock the live-index nested tests and give CI coverage of the full executeCollocations→logDice pipeline. Use @Assumptions.assumeTrue as a fallback so the tests still skip gracefully when the index is absent.
evidence: BlackLabQueryExecutorTest.LiveIndex is annotated @Disabled("Requires a live BlackLab index — set CONCEPT_SKETCH_TEST_INDEX env var and remove @Disabled to run")

The critical production paths — executeSurfacePattern, executeDependencyPattern, executeBcqlQuery — are exercised only through static helper unit tests (buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution) and stub-based exploration tests, never against a real index in CI
No small synthetic test index is built as a Maven test resource; BlackLabConllUIndexerTest presumably requires an external corpus

issues.summary: WordSketchApiServer convenience constructor constructs SemanticFieldExplorer inside the API layer
issues.suggestion: Move the construction of SemanticFieldExplorer out of WordSketchApiServer and into Main.handleBlackLabServerCommand, then remove the convenience constructor. Main.java is already the composition root and the natural place for wiring. This preserves the full-injection constructor as the only public entry point and eliminates the api→exploration construction coupling.
evidence: The convenience constructor 'public WordSketchApiServer(QueryExecutor executor, int port, GrammarConfig grammarConfig) throws IOException' calls 'new SemanticFieldExplorer(executor, grammarConfig)' directly, encoding exploration-layer construction rules inside the api package

The full constructor accepting an injected SemanticFieldExplorer exists and is correctly used in tests, but production startup in Main.java uses the convenience constructor — meaning Main→WordSketchApiServer→SemanticFieldExplorer is the actual wiring path
If SemanticFieldExplorer's constructor signature changes (e.g. gains a new dependency), WordSketchApiServer must also change even though it is nominally a different layer

issues.summary: api package blends HTTP infrastructure, response assembly, and config serialization without sub-package separation
issues.suggestion: Introduce sub-packages within api: api/http (HttpApiUtils, RequestEntityTooLargeException, WordSketchApiServer) and api/response (ExploreResponseAssembler, GrammarConfigSerializer). Handlers can remain at api/ level. This is a low-risk rename with no semantic change but meaningfully reduces cognitive width when navigating the package.
evidence: HttpApiUtils contains pure HTTP plumbing (sendJsonResponse, sendError, wrapWithErrorHandling, parseQueryParams) alongside RequestEntityTooLargeException

ExploreResponseAssembler converts model-layer ExplorationResult/ComparisonResult to JSON-ready maps — it is a presentation concern that depends on model objects but lives in the api package alongside the HTTP plumbing
GrammarConfigSerializer converts GrammarConfig→JSONObject for API responses — another presentation concern that could logically belong to config or a dedicated serialization sub-package
All 11 production api-package files are in one flat directory; a developer changing HTTP error handling must scan past response assemblers and config serializers to find the relevant files

issues.summary: GET endpoint query parameters (seed, lemma, adjective) have no length limit while POST endpoints enforce 65 KB
issues.suggestion: Add a MAX_PARAM_LENGTH constant (e.g. 200 characters) in HttpApiUtils and check it inside requireParam or in a new requireBoundedParam method. Apply it in ExplorationHandlers for seed/seeds/adjective/noun and in SketchHandlers for lemma. This mirrors the body-size discipline already applied to POST endpoints.
evidence: HttpApiUtils.readBodyWithSizeLimit enforces maxBytes for POST body reads, and both CorpusQueryHandlers and VisualizationHandlers call it with 65_536
HttpApiUtils.requireParam returns the raw trimmed string for GET parameters with no length check; a seed value of 50,000 characters passes through to SemanticFieldExplorer unchanged

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti accepts a comma-separated 'seeds' parameter and splits on commas — a pathologically long seeds list creates unbounded work in parseSeedSet and downstream query execution
Given the loopback-only deployment model this is low severity, but it creates an inconsistency: POST body size is guarded, GET parameter size is not
